title: Sourcing dried herbs
subtitle: What to look for in a reputable supplier
excerpt: A practical guide to buying dried herbs: what quality looks like, the difference between cut-and-sifted and powder, what to check on a supplier's label, and why sourcing matters for safety.
sourceNotes: herbal pharmacopoeia, general quality standards for dried herbal material. the European herbal standard framework for traditional herbal medicinal products, the quality and sourcing requirements. UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (the UK medicines regulator) guidance on herbal product quality, available at gov.uk.

glossaryTerms.definition: The standard preparation for dried loose herb material: the herb is dried, cut to a roughly uniform small size, and sieved to remove dust and fine powder. The most useful form for tinctures and decoctions.
The two-part Latin name for a plant species, consisting of genus and species. Verified Latin binomials on packaging confirm you are buying the intended herb and not a related but different species.

content.text: The quality of a dried herb determines the quality of everything made from it. A year-old powder that smells faintly of cardboard does not produce a useful herbal spirit extraction. An underfilled bottle from an unknown source may not contain what it says on the label. Sourcing well is not fastidious; it is the foundation on which every other preparation decision rests.
The correct form for your preparation
Most preparations in this library call for "

" dried herb, sometimes abbreviated C/S. This is dried herb cut to a roughly uniform small size and sifted to remove dust: good for tinctures (packs evenly into jars, strains cleanly), good for decoctions (simmers without turning to mush), and good for infusions (infuses evenly in a teapot). Powder (finely milled) extracts faster but produces a cloudy, difficult-to-strain preparation. Reserve powders for specific uses: slippery elm powder is used as a gruel; ashwagandha powder goes into warm milk. For tinctures and infusions, cut and sifted is the right form.
What the label should tell you
A good dried herb supplier will show: the common name, the

(genus and species), the part used (leaf, root, flower, aerial parts), the country of origin, and a best-before date. The Latin binomial is important because common names vary by region and can apply to multiple unrelated plants. "Elderflower" in Britain is Sambucus nigra; in some parts of North America, elder refers to related species with different safety profiles. The species name resolves the ambiguity.

Open the bag and smell the herb before buying, if the supplier allows, or within a few days of receiving an online order. Fresh dried herb smells strongly herbal, distinctly of the species. Chamomile should smell of sweet apples; peppermint of menthol; rosemary of camphor and pine; valerian of earth and ammonia (the smell is distinctive and not entirely pleasant, but it should be strong). A herb that smells faintly or of dust is old and has lost its aromatic oil; it will produce a weak preparation. Return it.

Look: cut and sifted herb should look identifiable. Chamomile flowers should look like small dried daisies, not unidentifiable beige fragments. Peppermint leaf should show the characteristic serrated leaf edge. If you cannot recognise what the herb looks like in cut form, a simple search for the herb's appearance confirms whether the product looks right. Discoloured, very brown, or uniformly grey material is often old or has been stored badly.

Organic certification reduces pesticide exposure in the finished preparation and is worth seeking for herbs that will be taken therapeutically over weeks or months. For one-off preparations or external use, the practical difference is small. Wildcrafted (foraged from wild plants) is sometimes said to be better than cultivated. It is not, and some wild-harvested species are over-harvested or harder to identify reliably. Cultivated herb from a good supplier is the safer and more consistent choice.

Most dried herbs keep 12 months in a sealed tin, cool, dark, and dry. Roots and bark often keep longer (18-24 months); flowers and delicate aerial parts are better used within 12 months. The enemy of dried herbs is light, heat, and moisture. Never store in a transparent jar on a sunny kitchen shelf. Metal tins with tight-fitting lids, stored in a cupboard away from the cooker and dishwasher, are the right containers. Label each tin with the herb, the source, the purchase date, and the best-before date. Use the oldest stock first.
